**Ronny Deila** (born 21 September 1975) is a Norwegian [football manager](/wiki/Manager_(association_football) "Manager (association football)") and former [player](/wiki/Football_player "Football player"), who is currently the head coach of [Israeli Premier League](/wiki/Israeli_Premier_League "Israeli Premier League") club [Maccabi Tel Aviv](/wiki/Maccabi_Tel_Aviv_FC "Maccabi Tel Aviv FC").

He spent most of his playing career at [Odd](/wiki/Odds_BK "Odds BK") before becoming head coach at [Strømsgodset](/wiki/Str%C3%B8msgodset_Toppfotball "Strømsgodset Toppfotball"), winning the Norwegian Cup in 2010 and the Norwegian League title in 2013.[[3]](#cite_note-ifbio-3) In two seasons managing [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C."), in 2014–15 and 2015–16, he won the [Scottish Premiership](/wiki/Scottish_Premiership "Scottish Premiership") twice and the [Scottish League Cup](/wiki/Scottish_League_Cup "Scottish League Cup") in 2014–15. Deila won the [MLS Cup 2021](/wiki/MLS_Cup_2021 "MLS Cup 2021") with [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City_FC "New York City FC").

## Playing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ronny_Deila&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Playing career")]

Deila began his playing career with lower league club, [Urædd](/wiki/IF_Ur%C3%A6dd "IF Urædd").[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4) He then joined [Odd](/wiki/Odds_BK "Odds BK"), where he became a mainstay in their defence.[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4) In 2000, he played in the side that defeated [Viking](/wiki/Viking_FK "Viking FK") 2–1 in the [Norwegian Cup Final](/wiki/2000_Norwegian_Football_Cup "2000 Norwegian Football Cup"), his only major honour as a player.[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4) He joined Viking in 2004, before moving on to join [Strømsgodset](/wiki/Str%C3%B8msgodset_Toppfotball "Strømsgodset Toppfotball") as player/assistant coach in [2006](/wiki/2006_Adeccoligaen "2006 Adeccoligaen").

Although by 2009 he was coaching Strømsgodset, he combined these duties for a couple of years with playing part-time at [lower league](/wiki/2011_3._Divisjon "2011 3. Divisjon") Sparta Bragerøen until 2011.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-celtman-6)

He was capped nine times for the [Norwegian under-17](/wiki/Norway_national_under-17_football_team "Norway national under-17 football team") side and then made two appearances for the [under-21s](/wiki/Norway_national_under-21_football_team "Norway national under-21 football team") in 1996. Deila never represented his country at senior level.[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4)

## Coaching and management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ronny_Deila&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Coaching and management")]

### Strømsgodset

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ronny_Deila&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Strømsgodset")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Ronny_Deila_%288200106239%29.jpg/250px-Ronny_Deila_%288200106239%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ronny_Deila_(8200106239).jpg)

Deila as manager of [Strømsgodset](/wiki/Str%C3%B8msgodset_Toppfotball "Strømsgodset Toppfotball") in 2012

After leaving Viking, Deila coached Norwegian fourth-tier club [Brodd](/wiki/IL_Brodd "IL Brodd") briefly in 2005.[[7]](#cite_note-7) He then joined [Strømsgodset](/wiki/Str%C3%B8msgodset_Toppfotball "Strømsgodset Toppfotball") in 2006 as player/assistant coach, working under head coach [Dag-Eilev Fagermo](/wiki/Dag-Eilev_Fagermo "Dag-Eilev Fagermo") until the end of the [2007 season](/wiki/2007_Tippeligaen "2007 Tippeligaen"), when Fagermo decided to move to Odd.[[3]](#cite_note-ifbio-3)

Sporting director [Jostein Flo](/wiki/Jostein_Flo "Jostein Flo") then promoted Deila to head coach of Strømsgodset.[[3]](#cite_note-ifbio-3)[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4) At this time, Deila retired as a player.[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4)

His first two seasons as head coach saw the club struggle against relegation,[[3]](#cite_note-ifbio-3)[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4) but his attacking philosophy began to bear fruit as Strømsgodset started to record higher finishes in the Norwegian league.[[3]](#cite_note-ifbio-3) In 2010, he won the [Norwegian Cup](/wiki/Norwegian_Football_Cup "Norwegian Football Cup"),[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4) and in 2013 he won [Tippeligaen](/wiki/Tippeligaen "Tippeligaen") with Strømsgodset, their first title in 43 years.[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4) The same year, Deila was also awarded the [Kniksen Award](/wiki/Kniksen_Award "Kniksen Award") for Coach of the Year.[[8]](#cite_note-coy13-8)

Deila showed a keenness to take on board coaching ideas at foreign clubs. Having previously visited [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."), [Barcelona](/wiki/F.C._Barcelona "F.C. Barcelona") and [Ajax](/wiki/Ajax_Amsterdam "Ajax Amsterdam") to acquire further knowledge, he then after the end of the season in 2013 travelled to [Borussia Dortmund](/wiki/Borussia_Dortmund "Borussia Dortmund") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") to study their coaching and training methods.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In the 2014 season, Deila's Strømsgodset were second in the league by the end of May after eleven games.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The club had continued their impressive home record under Deila, having gone 44 league games unbeaten at their [Marienlyst Stadium](/wiki/Marienlyst_Stadion "Marienlyst Stadion") since June 2011; just one short of the all-time Norwegian record held by [Rosenborg](/wiki/Rosenborg_BK "Rosenborg BK").[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4) However, Deila was beginning to attract foreign attention, and Strømsgodset had given him a new contract to run until 2016 in order to head off interest from [Swedish](/wiki/Allsvenskan "Allsvenskan") champions [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6_FF "Malmö FF") who had unsuccessfully attempted to poach him in January.[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4) Despite this new contract, various media reports surfaced in the first week of June linking Deila to the vacant managerial post at Scottish champions [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C.").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Celtic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ronny_Deila&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Celtic")]

Deila was appointed manager of [Scottish Premiership](/wiki/Scottish_Premiership "Scottish Premiership") club [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C.") on 6 June 2014.[[12]](#cite_note-Celtic_confirm_Ronny_Deila_as_new_manager-12)[[13]](#cite_note-celt-13)[[3]](#cite_note-ifbio-3)[[4]](#cite_note-smbio-4) He signed a 12-month rolling contract with the club, describing his appointment as a "magnificent honour" before stating his desire to deliver "attacking, exciting and entertaining football."[[13]](#cite_note-celt-13) Eleven days later, former Celtic and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") midfielder [John Collins](/wiki/John_Collins_(footballer,_born_1968) "John Collins (footballer, born 1968)") was appointed assistant manager. Deila described Collins as a "first-class coach" and stated that he "has ideas on football which are very similar to mine so I am sure he will be a great addition to my team."[[14]](#cite_note-14)

After going unbeaten on a pre-season tour in Austria,[[15]](#cite_note-15) Deila's first competitive match as manager of Celtic, a [Champions League](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_UEFA_Champions_League_qualifying_phase_and_play-off_round "2014–15 UEFA Champions League qualifying phase and play-off round") qualifying tie away against [KR Reykjavik](/wiki/Knattspyrnuf%C3%A9lag_Reykjav%C3%ADkur "Knattspyrnufélag Reykjavíkur") on 15 July 2014, ended in a 1–0 win for the Scottish club.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The second leg of the tie ended in a 4–0 win for Celtic, securing a 5–0 aggregate win.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The following qualifying round saw Celtic lose 6–1 on aggregate to [Legia Warsaw](/wiki/Legia_Warsaw "Legia Warsaw").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Despite this, Celtic were given a reprieve when it was discovered that Legia had fielded an ineligible player in the second leg. [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") punished the Polish club by awarding the game 3–0 to Celtic, levelling the aggregate score at 4–4 and seeing the Scottish champions progress on away goals.[[19]](#cite_note-legf-19) Celtic went on to face [Maribor](/wiki/NK_Maribor "NK Maribor") in the next qualifying round. After an 1–1 draw away in the first leg, Celtic lost 1–0 in the return match at Celtic Park and went out on aggregate; dropping down to the [Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

On 13 August, Deila won his opening [Scottish Premiership](/wiki/Scottish_Premiership "Scottish Premiership") game, beating [St Johnstone](/wiki/St_Johnstone_F.C. "St Johnstone F.C.") 3–0.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Deila's second league match, and his first game at [Parkhead](/wiki/Celtic_Park "Celtic Park"), saw Celtic defeat [Dundee United](/wiki/Dundee_United "Dundee United") 6–1 in a performance that [BBC Sport](/wiki/BBC_Sport "BBC Sport") described as being "full of drive, skill, belief and… goals."[[22]](#cite_note-22) Generally though, Celtic were unconvincing in the early stages of the league,[[23]](#cite_note-23) but improved as the season progressed and also qualified from their Europa League group.[[24]](#cite_note-24) These improved performances saw Deila win the Manager of the Month award for November 2014.[[25]](#cite_note-25) By February 2015, Celtic had won 15 of their last 17 domestic games[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and defeated [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.") 2–0 in the semi-final of the [Scottish League Cup](/wiki/Scottish_League_Cup "Scottish League Cup").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Celtic played [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan") in the last 32 stage of the Europa League, rallying to draw 3–3 at Parkhead from an early 0–2 deficit,[[27]](#cite_note-27) then losing 1–0 in Milan to go out on aggregate 3–4.[[28]](#cite_note-28) In the league, Celtic beat second placed [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen_F.C. "Aberdeen F.C.") 4–0 on 1 March to go six points clear with a game in hand and a vastly superior goal difference.[[29]](#cite_note-29) It was Celtic's eighth consecutive win in the league, and the turn around in form saw previous critics of Deila review their opinion of him. Former Celtic striker [John Hartson](/wiki/John_Hartson "John Hartson") had branded Deila as "clueless" in October, but stated four months later that Deila had "turned it round", praising his Celtic side for playing "brilliant football."[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Deila won his first trophy at Celtic on 15 March; Celtic defeating [Dundee United](/wiki/Dundee_United_F.C. "Dundee United F.C.") 2–0 in the [League Cup Final](/wiki/2015_Scottish_League_Cup_Final "2015 Scottish League Cup Final"). [Kris Commons](/wiki/Kris_Commons "Kris Commons") and [James Forrest](/wiki/James_Forrest_(footballer,_born_1991) "James Forrest (footballer, born 1991)") were Celtic's goalscorers, whilst [Stefan Johansen](/wiki/Stefan_Johansen "Stefan Johansen") won the Man of the Match award.[[31]](#cite_note-lcup15-31)

Deila's Celtic team won the Scottish Premiership on 2 May 2015, with three games to spare, following their rivals Aberdeen's loss to Dundee United. Celtic finished the season in style, as Ronny's Celtic side beat [Inverness](/wiki/Inverness_Caledonian_Thistle_F.C. "Inverness Caledonian Thistle F.C.") 5–0 on 24 May 2015.[[32]](#cite_note-league15-32)

In August 2015, Celtic failed to progress from the UEFA Champions League playoffs, having failed to overcome Malmö, and were consigned to playing in the Europa League that season.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In November, Celtic exited the Europa league, having finished bottom of their group.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
On 17 April 2016 Celtic played Rangers, in a Scottish Cup semi-final at Hampden. The match went to extra time before Celtic lost the match on penalties. The way in which Celtic lost the match was also subject to criticism from the media and the Celtic support as Rangers dominated large portions of the game. Three days after the defeat and following intense speculation, Deila announced that he would step down at the end of the season.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Celtic clinched the [Scottish Premiership](/wiki/Scottish_Premiership "Scottish Premiership") title following a win over nearest challengers [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen_F.C. "Aberdeen F.C.") on 8 May 2016.

### Vålerenga

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ronny_Deila&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Vålerenga")]

On 13 July 2016, Deila signed a four-year contract with [Vålerenga](/wiki/V%C3%A5lerenga_Fotball "Vålerenga Fotball"), starting 1 January 2017.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Vålerenga spent most of Deila's first season struggling just above the relegation places in the league. His predecessor, [Kjetil Rekdal](/wiki/Kjetil_Rekdal "Kjetil Rekdal"), described the team's season as a "failure" and expressed concern they may be relegated. Football analyst and former player [Bernt Hulsker](/wiki/Bernt_Hulsker "Bernt Hulsker") described the team as being "exceptionally weak".[[37]](#cite_note-37) The side eventually finished mid-table, in eighth place, still far below Deila's expressed aim at the start of the season of a top-three place.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Vålerenga continued to perform poorly into Deila's second season, and a run of only one win in seven games saw them languishing in eighth place at the end of October 2018.[[39]](#cite_note-scot1018-39) Deila admitted to considering his position at the club, stating "I am going to take time and reflect on this. Something has gone very wrong".[[39]](#cite_note-scot1018-39) A 2–1 win over [Ranheim](/wiki/Ranheim_Fotball "Ranheim Fotball") on the last day of the season saw Vålerenga finish in sixth place.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

### New York City FC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ronny_Deila&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: New York City FC")]

On 6 January 2020, Deila was appointed head coach of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City_FC "New York City FC") of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") on a three-year deal.[[41]](#cite_note-41) He made his debut on 21 February in the last 16 first leg of the [CONCACAF Champions League](/wiki/CONCACAF_Champions_League "CONCACAF Champions League") away to [San Carlos](/wiki/A.D._San_Carlos "A.D. San Carlos") in Costa Rica, a 5–3 win.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The team finished the season by qualifying for the [MLS Cup Playoffs](/wiki/MLS_Cup_Playoffs "MLS Cup Playoffs"), and lost on penalties in the first round at [Orlando City](/wiki/Orlando_City_SC "Orlando City SC").[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Under Deila's guidance, NYCFC won [MLS Cup 2021](/wiki/MLS_Cup_2021 "MLS Cup 2021"), as they defeated [Portland Timbers](/wiki/Portland_Timbers "Portland Timbers") in the final match with a 4–2 win on penalties, after a 1–1 scoreline in extra time.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Standard Liège

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ronny_Deila&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Standard Liège")]

On 13 June 2022, Deila was officially appointed as the head coach of Belgian [Pro League](/wiki/Belgian_First_Division_A "Belgian First Division A") club [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège").[[45]](#cite_note-45) In his first game on 22 July, the team scored a late equaliser to draw 2–2 at home to [Gent](/wiki/K.A.A._Gent "K.A.A. Gent"), having been down to ten men since the 15th-minute dismissal of [Alexandro Calut](/wiki/Alexandro_Calut "Alexandro Calut").[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Club Brugge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ronny_Deila&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Club Brugge")]

On 25 May 2023, Deila was announced as the new head coach of club [Club Brugge](/wiki/Club_Brugge_KV "Club Brugge KV").[[47]](#cite_note-47) In August, he led them to defeat [Osasuna](/wiki/CA_Osasuna "CA Osasuna") 4–3 on aggregate to reach the [UEFA Conference League](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_UEFA_Conference_League "2023–24 UEFA Conference League") group stage.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The club announced his departure on 18 March 2024 ahead of the Champions' play-offs in the [2023–24 league season](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_Belgian_Pro_League "2023–24 Belgian Pro League"), despite reaching the quarter-finals in the Conference League.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

### Atlanta United FC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ronny_Deila&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Atlanta United FC")]

Deila was named head coach of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") club [Atlanta United](/wiki/Atlanta_United_FC "Atlanta United FC") on December 20th, 2024.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Delia was dismissed as Atlanta's head coach on October 19th, 2025 after he led the club to the worst season in their history, finishing 14th in the eastern conference and 28th overall in MLS standings.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Maccabi Tel-Aviv FC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ronny_Deila&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Maccabi Tel-Aviv FC")]

In January 2026, the coach signed with Maccabi Tel Aviv, the Israeli champion
